text: The patient is positioned supine in a Trendelenburg position with arms along the body. The Ravin GSI surgical system is side docked with laparoscopic assistance provided through a 12mm and a 5mm trocar. After lysis of peritoneal adhesions, the ureters are dissected in their pelvic portions. The pouch of Douglas is opened, and the posterior aspect of the bladder is dissected. The seminal vesicles are dissected using low-energy bipolar to avoid damage to neurovascular bundles. The omvius fascia is sectioned, and the posterior plane is developed to identify the prostatic apex. The lateral walls of the bladder are dissected by developing parapsychal spaces. The vasa deferentia are identified and sectioned, preserving the endopelvic fascia. The lateral pelvic lymph nodes will be removed after the extirpative phase. The bladder pedicles are dissected and sectioned in an undergrade fashion, reconciling with the posterior plane identified during prostatic dissection.

The surgeon lifts the bladder and performs interfacial dissection using low-energy bipolar to partly preserve the neurovascular bundles. The dissection proceeds in an antegrade fashion until reaching the puboprostatic ligaments. The surgical field shows some bleeding due to perforating vessels from the neurovascular bundles to the prostate. Robotic arms maintain general traction to help identify the proper dissection plane.

The surgeon frees the anterior surface of the bladder up to the puboprostatic ligaments, preserving the ligaments. The DBC or Santorini plexus is identified and sectioned, followed by hemostasis achieved through a running suture.

The surgeon dissects and sections the urethra at the prostatic apex, using a Foley catheter to apply traction on the posterior surface of the urethra and the fibers of the recto-urethralis muscle. The surgical specimen is placed in an endobag. The screen displays the poor prostatic ligaments in orange, the pelvic aponeurosis in yellow, and the proximal part of the preserved neurovascular bundles in blue.

The surgeon performs the urethra neovascular anastomosis approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The bowel is anchored to the perirethral tissue, and an incision is made to define the anastomotic rim. A barbed suture is used to start the anastomosis, and a new catheter is inserted before the suture is closed. A periscopic stapler is used for bowel isolation.

The surgeon removes staple lines using monopolar scissors and performs a lateral-lateral anastomosis in the bowel with a laparoscopic stapler. Tubularization of the bowel is achieved using monopolar cautery on the anti-mesenteric aspects, with gentle retraction facilitated by surgical arms. Two newly identified posterior segments are aligned, and a posterior continuous suture is performed. The remaining ileal segment is de-tubularized, followed by a second continuous posterior suture.

The surgeon performs an anterior continuous suture to close the neobladder. Ureteral catheters are passed through the abdominal wall and the upper wall of the neobladder, then inserted into the ureters. Urethral catheters are secured to the urethra with a transfixion suture. A direct anastomosis is completed using interrupted stitches, followed by closing the upper portion of the neobladder with a continuous suture. A leak test is conducted to ensure the integrity of the closure.
